title: The Signal-Weather Ceiling

features.name: antenna-breakthrough
features.desc-on-touch: The antenna has broken through the ground and reached the sky. You cannot tell if the weather is interrupting the signal or the signal is calling the weather down.

features.name: weather-interface
features.desc-on-touch: At the threshold between broadcast and storm, the archive learns that signal and weather speak the same language. Both arrive as noise. Both carry meaning.

features.name: signal-scatter
features.desc-on-touch: The air is full of things that used to be broadcast. Now they drift like weather. The archive is learning to read wind as a continuous transmission.

features.name: sky-marking
features.desc-on-touch: Pale marks on the dark sky. The archive has begun annotating weather patterns, filing them as text. The forecast becomes a documentary.

features.name: wind-hum
features.desc-on-touch: The pole sings. The wind has learned frequencies. The archive taught it by mistake, broadcasting long enough for the air to memorize the shape of sound.

inscription: the ceiling is not solid; it is only the place where signal decided it could also be rain

file.name: Weather_Signal_Convergence_Logbook.txt
file.note: Where the antenna breaks through, broadcasts and storm systems become indistinguishable. The archive reads both as texts from the sky.

exits.label: back down to the station where the antenna still has roots
north where the weather continues beyond any signal
exits.to: NEW: the storm's archive, what weather remembers when radio falls silent

exits.label: east where the wind carries older broadcasts
west where the signal is still learning to be weather
exits.to: NEW: the transmission-end where weather begins its own broadcasting

reverie.title: REVERIE — raising the antenna in wind
narr: You hold the pole while Katita climbs. Rain begins.
The antenna cuts through drops like a needle through cloth.
She shouts something but the wind is already broadcasting it.
You cannot tell if she is talking or the sky is speaking her words.
Below you both, the archive's batteries drink the weather.
The broadcast and the storm have always been one transmission.
